title: Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome
oneLiner: Acute neuropsychiatric emergency from thiamine deficiency; mainly in alcohol misuse but also malnutrition, hyperemesis, bariatric surgery; untreated → death or permanent amnesia.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute hypoglycaemia (alcohol-related or otherwise):** check capillary glucose immediately — confusion + ataxia can look identical. If glucose <3.0 mmol/L → give 50 mL 50% dextrose IV.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** asterixis, foetor hepaticus, jaundice + confusion — lactulose, not thiamine, is first line. Differentiate: Wernicke’s usually has eye signs (nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia); hepatic encephalopathy does not.
- **Subdural haematoma (fall from ataxia):** unilateral focal signs, progressive drowsiness — CT is critical if any asymmetry or trauma history.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (the triad — present in <30%):**
  - Acute confusion / disorientation (global, not just alcohol intoxication)
  - Ataxia (broad-based gait, often unable to walk)
  - Ophthalmoplegia / nystagmus (horizontal or vertical nystagmus, lateral rectus palsy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Nystagmus (most common eye sign) or conjugate gaze palsy
  - Confusion without focal neurology (may be subtle — check orientation, attention)
  - Hypothermia, hypotension (autonomic)
  - Peripheral neuropathy (may co-exist)
  - Signs of malnutrition (glossitis, angular stomatitis, low BMI)
- **Red flag:** any patient with alcohol history and ANY confusion/ataxia/eye sign → treat presumptively.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Alcohol intoxication:** only confusional state, no nystagmus, resolves with time. **Splitter:** nystagmus or ataxia persists after blood alcohol <0.08% → Wernicke’s.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** asterixis, jaundice, elevated NH₃, no eye signs.
- **Hypoglycaemia:** check glucose — mimics all features.
- **Stroke (posterior circulation):** sudden onset, gaze palsy/pupillary asymmetry, vertigo — but no global confusion. CT or MRI confirms.
- **Meningoencephalitis:** fever, photophobia, CSF pleocytosis.
- **Intracranial bleed:** trauma history, focal signs, headache.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose, ECG (prolonged QTc if hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia), pulse oximetry, urine dip for ketones (if DKA possible).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation, glucose
  - Serum magnesium (hypomagnesaemia common → thiamine resistant)
  - Phosphate (refeeding risk if starting nutrition)
  - Blood alcohol level (if unclear)
  - Thiamine level (rarely urgent — treat before results)
- **Imaging:**
  - Head CT (to rule out bleed) if any focal signs, trauma, or anticoagulation
  - MRI brain (if diagnosis uncertain or atypical features) — T2/FLAIR shows high signal in periaqueductal grey, medial thalamus, mammillary bodies
- **Special tests:** CSF only if suspect meningitis (CT first)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Caine criteria** (requires ≥2 of: dietary deficiency, eye signs, cerebellar signs, confusion/memory impairment) — high sensitivity for encephalopathy.
- **Never-miss test:** **Capillary glucose** — treat hypoglycaemia immediately before anything else. Also **serum magnesium** (hypomagnesaemia makes thiamine ineffective).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Give **IV thiamine** (Pabrinex) immediately **before** any carbohydrate/glucose — glucose administration without thiamine can precipitate acute Wernicke’s.
  - If hypoglycaemic: treat with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV **only after**/simultaneously with thiamine.
  - Correct hypomagnesaemia (Mg >0.85 mmol/L needed for thiamine action) – give MgSO₄ IV if <0.7 mmol/L.
  - Monitor NEWS2, 1:1 nursing if agitated.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV Pabrinex 1 pair twice daily for 3–5 days (or until clinical improvement).
  - Convert to oral thiamine 100 mg BD–TDS after IV course.
  - Monitor for refeeding syndrome if restarting nutrition (check K+, Mg, phosphate daily).
  - Assess for alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar protocol) — treat with benzodiazepines if needed.
  - Multidisciplinary: dietician, addiction services, occupational therapy (cognitive assessment).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Oral thiamine 100 mg BD + multivitamin B complex indefinitely if ongoing alcohol use.
  - Abstinence from alcohol (counselling, naltrexone/acamprosate if appropriate).
  - Neuropsychology follow-up if memory deficits persist.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 5% glucose with Pabrinex (or normal saline if dehydrated) – but avoid dextrose-only bags (cannot give thiamine in dextrose; use separate lines).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (unless bleeding risk, e.g., alcoholic liver disease with coagulopathy).
  - Diet: normal ward diet (oral supplements if malnourished) — avoid high-carb boluses early.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly, GCS 1–2 hourly initially.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent:** **Pabrinex** (IV pair of ampoules 1 + 2). Dose: 1 pair (250 mg thiamine + 50 mg pyridoxine + 4 mg riboflavin) in 100 mL N/Saline or 5% glucose over 30 min. Frequency: twice daily for 3–5 days.
- **Alternative:** IV thiamine alone not available in UK as single agent in routine stock; Pabrinex is standard.
- **Key contraindications:** Known hypersensitivity to thiamine or any component (rare). **Do not give as IV bolus undiluted** — risk of anaphylaxis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None required. Use with caution in severe hepatic encephalopathy (other sedatives may worsen).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving glucose-containing fluids without Pabrinex first** — can precipitate acute Wernicke’s encephalopathy in thiamine-deficient patients. Also **underdosing** — single doses are insufficient in active encephalopathy.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Worsening encephalopathy → coma, death
  - Seizures (if also alcohol withdrawal)
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (if hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia uncorrected)
  - Anaphylaxis (very rare with Pabrinex but real — have adrenaline available)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Korsakoff syndrome:** irreversible amnesia (anterograde more than retrograde), confabulation, apathy — often permanent even with treatment
  - Peripheral neuropathy (if co-existing B1 deficiency)
  - Ataxia that may not fully resolve
sections.Prognosis: - Untreated acute Wernicke’s: mortality ~20% (often from infection or autonomic instability). With prompt IV thiamine: 80% show improvement of eye signs within hours; confusion improves over days. However, up to 80% develop Korsakoff syndrome if diagnosis delayed – only 25% recover fully.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Treat before you diagnose:** If any alcohol history + confusion/ataxia/eye sign → give Pabrinex immediately. CT/MRI can wait.
- **Check magnesium** – if low (<0.85 mmol/L), give IV MgSO₄ 4g over 4 hours before thiamine will work.
- **Document the Caine criteria** in the notes – it shows you recognised the syndrome and justifies treatment to seniors/auditors.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never give glucose alone** (e.g., 50% dextrose for hypoglycaemia) without first or simultaneously giving Pabrinex.
- **Don’t stop Pabrinex too early** – the IV course must be at least 3 days (ideally 5) even if the patient looks better; otherwise relapse is common.
- **Don’t assume it’s just alcohol intoxication** – if nystagmus or ataxia persists after sobriety, treat as Wernicke’s.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** confusion worse, new falls, double vision, vomiting, or inability to keep down oral thiamine.
- **Follow-up:** GP or community alcohol team in 1 week; neuropsychology referral if any memory complaints persist.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior immediately if:** GCS drops by ≥2 points, new focal neurology, seizure, NEWS2 ≥7, or if unable to tolerate IV Pabrinex (phlebitis, anaphylaxis).
- **ICU referral:** persistent coma (GCS <8), need for airway protection, refractory seizures, or severe autonomic instability (hypotension/hypothermia).

rawMarkdown:
## Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome
**One-liner:** Acute neuropsychiatric emergency from thiamine deficiency; mainly in alcohol misuse but also malnutrition, hyperemesis, bariatric surgery; untreated → death or permanent amnesia.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute hypoglycaemia (alcohol-related or otherwise):** check capillary glucose immediately — confusion + ataxia can look identical. If glucose <3.0 mmol/L → give 50 mL 50% dextrose IV.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** asterixis, foetor hepaticus, jaundice + confusion — lactulose, not thiamine, is first line. Differentiate: Wernicke’s usually has eye signs (nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia); hepatic encephalopathy does not.
- **Subdural haematoma (fall from ataxia):** unilateral focal signs, progressive drowsiness — CT is critical if any asymmetry or trauma history.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (the triad — present in <30%):**
  - Acute confusion / disorientation (global, not just alcohol intoxication)
  - Ataxia (broad-based gait, often unable to walk)
  - Ophthalmoplegia / nystagmus (horizontal or vertical nystagmus, lateral rectus palsy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Nystagmus (most common eye sign) or conjugate gaze palsy
  - Confusion without focal neurology (may be subtle — check orientation, attention)
  - Hypothermia, hypotension (autonomic)
  - Peripheral neuropathy (may co-exist)
  - Signs of malnutrition (glossitis, angular stomatitis, low BMI)
- **Red flag:** any patient with alcohol history and ANY confusion/ataxia/eye sign → treat presumptively.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Alcohol intoxication:** only confusional state, no nystagmus, resolves with time. **Splitter:** nystagmus or ataxia persists after blood alcohol <0.08% → Wernicke’s.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** asterixis, jaundice, elevated NH₃, no eye signs.
- **Hypoglycaemia:** check glucose — mimics all features.
- **Stroke (posterior circulation):** sudden onset, gaze palsy/pupillary asymmetry, vertigo — but no global confusion. CT or MRI confirms.
- **Meningoencephalitis:** fever, photophobia, CSF pleocytosis.
- **Intracranial bleed:** trauma history, focal signs, headache.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose, ECG (prolonged QTc if hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia), pulse oximetry, urine dip for ketones (if DKA possible).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation, glucose
  - Serum magnesium (hypomagnesaemia common → thiamine resistant)
  - Phosphate (refeeding risk if starting nutrition)
  - Blood alcohol level (if unclear)
  - Thiamine level (rarely urgent — treat before results)
- **Imaging:**
  - Head CT (to rule out bleed) if any focal signs, trauma, or anticoagulation
  - MRI brain (if diagnosis uncertain or atypical features) — T2/FLAIR shows high signal in periaqueductal grey, medial thalamus, mammillary bodies
- **Special tests:** CSF only if suspect meningitis (CT first)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Caine criteria** (requires ≥2 of: dietary deficiency, eye signs, cerebellar signs, confusion/memory impairment) — high sensitivity for encephalopathy.
- **Never-miss test:** **Capillary glucose** — treat hypoglycaemia immediately before anything else. Also **serum magnesium** (hypomagnesaemia makes thiamine ineffective).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Give **IV thiamine** (Pabrinex) immediately **before** any carbohydrate/glucose — glucose administration without thiamine can precipitate acute Wernicke’s.
  - If hypoglycaemic: treat with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV **only after**/simultaneously with thiamine.
  - Correct hypomagnesaemia (Mg >0.85 mmol/L needed for thiamine action) – give MgSO₄ IV if <0.7 mmol/L.
  - Monitor NEWS2, 1:1 nursing if agitated.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV Pabrinex 1 pair twice daily for 3–5 days (or until clinical improvement).
  - Convert to oral thiamine 100 mg BD–TDS after IV course.
  - Monitor for refeeding syndrome if restarting nutrition (check K+, Mg, phosphate daily).
  - Assess for alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar protocol) — treat with benzodiazepines if needed.
  - Multidisciplinary: dietician, addiction services, occupational therapy (cognitive assessment).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Oral thiamine 100 mg BD + multivitamin B complex indefinitely if ongoing alcohol use.
  - Abstinence from alcohol (counselling, naltrexone/acamprosate if appropriate).
  - Neuropsychology follow-up if memory deficits persist.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 5% glucose with Pabrinex (or normal saline if dehydrated) – but avoid dextrose-only bags (cannot give thiamine in dextrose; use separate lines).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (unless bleeding risk, e.g., alcoholic liver disease with coagulopathy).
  - Diet: normal ward diet (oral supplements if malnourished) — avoid high-carb boluses early.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly, GCS 1–2 hourly initially.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent:** **Pabrinex** (IV pair of ampoules 1 + 2). Dose: 1 pair (250 mg thiamine + 50 mg pyridoxine + 4 mg riboflavin) in 100 mL N/Saline or 5% glucose over 30 min. Frequency: twice daily for 3–5 days.
- **Alternative:** IV thiamine alone not available in UK as single agent in routine stock; Pabrinex is standard.
- **Key contraindications:** Known hypersensitivity to thiamine or any component (rare). **Do not give as IV bolus undiluted** — risk of anaphylaxis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None required. Use with caution in severe hepatic encephalopathy (other sedatives may worsen).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving glucose-containing fluids without Pabrinex first** — can precipitate acute Wernicke’s encephalopathy in thiamine-deficient patients. Also **underdosing** — single doses are insufficient in active encephalopathy.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Worsening encephalopathy → coma, death
  - Seizures (if also alcohol withdrawal)
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (if hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia uncorrected)
  - Anaphylaxis (very rare with Pabrinex but real — have adrenaline available)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Korsakoff syndrome:** irreversible amnesia (anterograde more than retrograde), confabulation, apathy — often permanent even with treatment
  - Peripheral neuropathy (if co-existing B1 deficiency)
  - Ataxia that may not fully resolve

### Prognosis
- Untreated acute Wernicke’s: mortality ~20% (often from infection or autonomic instability). With prompt IV thiamine: 80% show improvement of eye signs within hours; confusion improves over days. However, up to 80% develop Korsakoff syndrome if diagnosis delayed – only 25% recover fully.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Treat before you diagnose:** If any alcohol history + confusion/ataxia/eye sign → give Pabrinex immediately. CT/MRI can wait.
- **Check magnesium** – if low (<0.85 mmol/L), give IV MgSO₄ 4g over 4 hours before thiamine will work.
- **Document the Caine criteria** in the notes – it shows you recognised the syndrome and justifies treatment to seniors/auditors.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never give glucose alone** (e.g., 50% dextrose for hypoglycaemia) without first or simultaneously giving Pabrinex.
- **Don’t stop Pabrinex too early** – the IV course must be at least 3 days (ideally 5) even if the patient looks better; otherwise relapse is common.
- **Don’t assume it’s just alcohol intoxication** – if nystagmus or ataxia persists after sobriety, treat as Wernicke’s.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** confusion worse, new falls, double vision, vomiting, or inability to keep down oral thiamine.
- **Follow-up:** GP or community alcohol team in 1 week; neuropsychology referral if any memory complaints persist.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior immediately if:** GCS drops by ≥2 points, new focal neurology, seizure, NEWS2 ≥7, or if unable to tolerate IV Pabrinex (phlebitis, anaphylaxis).
- **ICU referral:** persistent coma (GCS <8), need for airway protection, refractory seizures, or severe autonomic instability (hypotension/hypothermia).
